text: Describe a case of left laparoscopic adenectomy in a forty-year-old lady whose hypothyroid came with weak abdominal pain which was generalized in nature. Her ultrasound was normal, so she was asked for a CT scan in view of her persistent pain, which showed a lesion in the left adrenal which was heterogeneous and cystic in nature, suggestive of an adrenal adenoma. Her further lab investigations were performed, which showed that her thyroid function tests were normal, her ACTH and basal cortisol levels were normal, and the rest of her investigations were normal, suggestive that this was a non-secretory adrenal tumor. With this plan in mind, we had planned for laparoscopic left adrenalectomy.

The video shows a CT scan image of the left adrenal adenoma, clearly demarcated. The surgical setup includes the patient in the left lateral position with a four-port technique. Visible are two 5mm ports, a 10mm port, and a 12mm optical port. The initial optical entry is established, followed by the placement of an additional right 5mm port and a left 5mm port. An additional 5mm port is placed in the lower quadrant to assist in mobilizing the spleen and stomach.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the splenic flexure, carefully dissecting to lower the colon and prevent injury. The spleen and pancreas are gradually moved to the right side to expose the left adrenal gland. Gauze is used to reflect the spleen, aiding in its mobilization. As dissection continues, the pancreas is moved out of the way, and the upper part of the stomach fundus is exposed to prevent injury during gland manipulation.

The surgeon exposes the left adrenal gland, identifying the inferior phrenic vein and following it to locate the left adrenal vein. Sharp and blunt dissection is used to isolate the adrenal vein, which is then clipped and divided. Additional blood vessels supplying the adrenal are clipped and cut, leaving the inferior phrenic vein as the sole intact blood supply. The adrenal gland is mobilized from the retroperitoneum using a harmonic scalpel, with no blood loss observed. An arterial twig is separated and either clipped or sacrificed with the harmonic scalpel. The adrenal gland is gradually disconnected from its lower and lateral attachments, ultimately hanging by the inferior phrenic vein, which is clipped to complete the dissection and removal.

The surgeon removes the adrenal gland specimen using an endogenous plastic bag through the twelve millimeter port with a gallbladder extractor. The surgical bed is shown to be free of any bleeding and the spleen is repositioned to prevent torsion injuries. The specimen is visibly extracted from the port.
And that is the surgical team which helped me through my procedure. This was performed at the Department of General Surgery, KM Hospital, Mumbai. Thank you very much.
